coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots from black soya beans for free radical scavenging, Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Jing
Lin, Bo
Gao, Yifang
Jiao, Yuan
Li, Lei
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: The novel N doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were readily fabricated through one-step pyrolysis method from black soya beans. The obtained N-CDs possessed excellent photoluminescence properties with quantum yield of 38.7 ± 0.64% and dual responsive properties towards free radical and Fe3+. The N-CDs exhibited favorable radical scavenging activity (RSA) against 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and superoxide anion radicals. Fe3+ could induce photoluminescence quenching of N-CDs through static quenching characteristics. The possible quenching mechanism was presented. Combining with negligible toxicity and preferable biocompatibility, the N-CDs were extended to imaging intracellular Fe3+. As an attractive candidate, the N-CDs demonstrated a great potential in free radical scavenging, sensing and cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly luminescent
N-doped carbon dots
Radical scavenging activity
Fe3+ detection
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DPPH Free Radical Inhibition Activity Assessment
In Vitro Superoxide Anion Inhibition Activity Assessment
Fluorimetry for the Determination of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and (B) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (C) XPS spectrum, and (D) C1s, (E) O1s, and (F) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the N-CDs.

(A) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. Inset: photographs of the corresponding samples under sunlight and UV light. (B) Excitation dependent emission spectra of N-CDs. (C) Effect of pH, (D) ionic strength, (E) time intervals of irradiation with xenon arc light, (F) storage time on the PL intensity at 470 nm of N-CDs.
(A) Absorbance titration spectra of DPPH upon gradual addition of N-CDs from 0 to 300 μg·mL−1. (B) Scavenging ability of the N-CDs against DPPH and superoxide anion radicals.

(A) The changes in fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs upon addition of various metal ions. (C) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0.2–300 μM). The inset shows the photos of the N-CDs in the absence and presence Fe3+ under UV light of 365 nm. (D) The linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration. (The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements).

(A) Fluorescence decay traces of N-CDs in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of Fe3+. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (0.35 mg·mL−1), Fe3+ (300 mM), and CDs/Fe3+ (the experimental curve). The theoretical curve was obtained by the physical addition of the spectrum of CD solution and Fe3+ solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cell viability values (%) estimated by MTT proliferation tests versus incubation concentrations of N-CDs at 37 °C for 24 h.

(A) Bright-field and (B–D) fluorescent images of SMMC-7721 cells incubated with 0.35 mg·mL−1 N-CDs for 3 h at 37 °C. The fluorescent images were obtained at the excitation wavelengths of (B) 405 nm, (C) 488 nm, and (D) 543 nm. (E–H) The fluorescence image of the N-CDs-pretreated SMMC-7721 cells incubation with different concentration (0, 50, 100, and 150 μM in the culture solution) Fe3+ for 0.5 h at 37 °C.
Illustration of the synthetic procedure of the N-CDs and their multiple application in free radical scavenging, Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging.
Radical scavenging activity mechanism of N-CDs.
Comparison of the quantum yield and sensing properties of the CDs synthesized from different biomass.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: –: No linear range and detection limit for DPPH and superoxide.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots from black soya beans for free radical scavenging, Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Petroleum Engineering, Swanson School of Engineering, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15261, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel N doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were readily fabricated through one-step pyrolysis method from black soya beans. The obtained N-CDs possessed excellent photoluminescence properties with quantum yield of 38.7 ± 0.64% and dual responsive properties towards free radical and Fe3+. The N-CDs exhibited favorable radical scavenging activity (RSA) against 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and superoxide anion radicals. Fe3+ could induce photoluminescence quenching of N-CDs through static quenching characteristics. The possible quenching mechanism was presented. Combining with negligible toxicity and preferable biocompatibility, the N-CDs were extended to imaging intracellular Fe3+. As an attractive candidate, the N-CDs demonstrated a great potential in free radical scavenging, sensing and cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as the emerging nanomaterials, have drawn extensive attention owing to their superior optical properties, extreme small size, excellent chemical inertness and high biocompatibility [1–3]. Among the various features, photoluminescence (PL) is one of the outstanding behaviors, which makes CDs apply in bioimaging [4–6], sensing [7–9], photocatalysis [10,11], and phototherapy [12,13]. Although the accurate mechanism of photoluminescence from CDs is kept unknown, it is widely believed that the size and surface configuration of CDs play a pivotal role in determining the optical properties [14]. Heteroatom doping of CDs is an effective approach to regulate their luminescence [15–17]. The participation of heteroatoms can modulate band gap and electronic density of CDs as well as increase the quantum yield (QY) of CDs [18]. Especially, nitrogen (N) often acts as a doping element to regulate the electronic properties of CDs [19]. A variety of synthesis strategies have been exploited to obtain N-CDs, which usually can be classified into two types [20]. One is to handle the as-prepared CDs with additional N sources [21–23]. In such method, multiple steps are involved, which will inevitably reduce the controllability over the compositions and the morphologies of the obtained N-CDs [24]. The other is to directly adopt N-containing substance as the precursors of CDs [25–27]. Comparing these two methods, the latter offers facile fabrication of fluorescent CDs without any surface passivation and provides more chance to regulate the structure and property of the N-CDs [28]. Thus, massive efforts have been made to prepare N-CDs with N-containing molecules (e.g., glucosamine hydrochloride [29], o-phenylenediamine [30], or polyvinylpyrrolidone [31]. However, all these mentioned chemical reagents fail to possess renewable nature, broad availability and low toxicity. For green chemistry concern, nature biomass is a good candidate as the raw material for preparation of CDs.

Biomass is rich in carbohydrates such as sugar, starch, and polysaccharides that supply amounts of C, H and O elements for the fabrication of CDs. Meanwhile, the biomass also contains proteins and other molecules that can provide N elements [32]. Liu et al. [33] firstly used readily available grass as the precursor to directly synthesize water-soluble, nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots (PPNDs) without any passivation agent. This research opens a novel dimension for preparing CDs using substance widely distributed in nature. Subsequently, other biomass such as winter melon [34], sweet potato [35], cornstalk [36], mangosteen pulp [37], soy milk [38], lotus root [39], pomelo peel [40] have also been successfully applied for CDs synthesis, demonstrating a great potential of biological sources. All these methods are free from use of harmful and toxic chemicals. Unfortunately, fluorescence quantum yields (QY) are not impressive (<15%), nailing down their down-to-earth applicability [41]. There are few reports on obtainable strong photoluminescence carbon dots from natural biomass. Sahu et al. [42] used orange juice as the sole precursor to prepare N-CDs with the quantum yield of 26%. A simple and green synthetic method towards preparing strong fluorescent CDs derived from pigskin have also been demonstrated by Wen et al. [43]. Jones's group [44] utilized chia seeds to synthesize a series of CDs with high quantum yield up to 25% by only adjusting the dispersion solvents. Considerable efforts of these pioneers are suggested that the nature biomass contains complex compositions, often causing unpredictable results due to the individual difference, further affecting the optical properties of CDs. Therefore, the exploration of new biomass is necessary to meet the need for CDs' practical applicability.

In the present study, we try to introduce black soya beans as sole raw material for preparing CDs. Black soya beans are available from market and contain 45.70% protein, 15.60% fat, multiple microelement and vitamins [45]. It is obvious that high level of nitrogen is present from its compositions. We anticipate more opportunities to synthesize N-CDs with excellent luminescence behavior. Herein, the N-CDs have been prepared from black soya beans without any surface passivation agent. The prepared N-CDs possessed good water dispersibility, highly photoluminescence properties with the quantum yield of 38.8%, and excellent chemical and optical stabilities. More strikingly, we found the N-CDs possess dual responsive properties towards Fe3+ and free radicals, such as DPPH and superoxide anion radicals. The intensity of free radical characteristic absorption decreased prominently in the presence of N-CDs. Fe3+ could induce photoluminescence quenching of N-CDs through static quenching characteristics. Those phenomena enabled N-CDs acted as the radical scavenger and Fe3+ sensor. Furthermore, the as-prepared N-CDs were successfully employed for cellular imaging and detection of Fe3+ in intracellular (Scheme 1 ).

The black soya beans were purchased from the vegetable market (Taiyuan, China). FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 1,2,3-Trihydroxybenzene, salicylic acid, H2O2, Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, ascorbic acid and other chemicals used in this work were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). SMMC-7721 cells were provided by the Gene Engineering Center of Shanxi University. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water (≥18.25 MU·cm−1) from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore) was used in all experiments.

N-CDs were synthesized through pyrolysis treatment of black soya beans. Briefly, 2 g of black soya beans were placed in a porcelain crucible and pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace. The carbonized products were then cooled to room temperature and mechanically grounded to fine powders. 0.2 g of the synthetic sample was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and magnetically stirred to form a black-brown solution. Large particles or precipitates in the solution were removed through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, the N-CDs aqueous solution were further purified through continuous dialysis to remove excess salt and other small molecules. Finally, the solution was freeze dried to obtain a brown solid, which were stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPPH Free Radical Inhibition Activity Assessment
ce:section.ce:para:
The DPPH free radical scavenging activity was measured according to the method described by the previous report, but with few modifications [46]. In the tests, 100 μL of the N-CDs solution with various concentrations were added to 2 mL of DPPH (100 μM) ethanol solution. The decrease in absorption was measured at 515 nm after incubation for 40 min in dark. The RSA towards DPPH was estimated by Eq. (1).
(1)
inhibition
%
=
A control
−
A sample
A control
100 %
where Acontrol and Asample refer to the absorbance of the DPPH at 515 nm in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro Superoxide Anion Inhibition Activity Assessment
ce:section.ce:para:
The ability of N-CDs to scavenge superoxide anion was carried out at 25 °C. 2850 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M, pH 8.2) was placed in a quartz cuvette. Then 100 μL N-CDs aqueous with diverse concentrations and 50 μL of pyrogallol solution (30 mM in 10 mM HCl) were sequentially added to the buffer. Immediately after the mixture was shaken vigorously, the reaction mixture was scanning once every 40 s for total 4 min and recording absorbance at 325 nm, using a UV-2910 spectrophotometer. The scavenging of superoxide anion radicals was determined according Eq. (2).
(2)
inhibition
%
=
Δ
A control
− Δ
A sample
Δ
A control
100 %
where ΔAcontrol and ΔAsample refer to the difference in absorbance between the fourth minute and the zero minute of the superoxide at 325 nm in the absence and presence of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetry for the Determination of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Individual different concentrations of 10 μL Fe3+ was separately added into the mixture of 100 μL of N-CDs solution (0.35 mg·mL−1) and 900 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with excitation wavelength at 360 nm after reaction for 3 min. The selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ was examined by adding 100 μM of other metal ions instead of Fe3+.

SMMC-7721 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and inoculated at 37 °C under 5.0% CO2 for 48 h. Then, the N-CDs solution with a concentration of 0.35 mg·mL−1 was added to the original culture medium. After incubation for 3 h, the culture medium with surplus N-CDs was then discarded and the SMMC-7721 cells were washed with 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) three times. Fluorescence imaging of cells were performed using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

For assessing Fe3+ uptake, the dynamic fluorescence imaging and semi-quantitative determination for Fe3+ in N-CDs-pretreated SMMC-7721 cells had been performed. SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with 0.35 mg·mL−1 N-CDs for 3 h as described above, followed by addition 10 μL of PBS (as control) or Fe3+ with a concentration of 10 mM. The dynamic SMMC-7721 cells images were monitored at various reaction times (0, 5, 10 and 15 min). For the semi-quantitative determination of Fe3+, the N-CDs-pretreated SMMC-7721 cells were followed by incubation with various concentrations (0, 50, 100 and 150 μM) Fe3+ for another 0.5 h. Then, the cells were rinsed with PBS for 3 times to remove extracellular superfluous N-CDs and Fe3+.

Morphology and size distribution of the N-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The N-CDs were homogeneously sphere shaped and well dispersed (Fig. 1A). The average diameter of the N-CDs was around 5.16 ± 0.30 nm (Fig. S1). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1A) showed that N-CDs possessed the crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was similar with the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [47].

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of N-CDs were performed by the elemental analysis, FTIR spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The elemental analysis revealed that the obtained N-CDs were mainly comprised of C 43.08 wt%, H 6.52 wt%, N 8.75 wt%, and O (calculated) 41.65 wt%. The FTIR spectrum was given in Fig. 1B. The wide peak at 3400 cm−1 aroused from stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The tiny band at 2931 cm−1 was due to CH vibrations. The peaks at 1655 and 1544 cm−1 indicated the existence of CO and CC, respectively. The band at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN. The peaks at 1242 and 1080 cm−1 indicated the existence of CO. The full range XPS (Fig. 1C) spectra exhibited three peaks at 283.5, 398.8, and 531.1 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 1D exhibited four main peaks at 284.5, 285.1, 286.1, and 287.4 eV, which could be ascribed to CC, CN, COH, CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1E, O1s could be divided into two peaks at 531.8 and 532.7 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) displayed double peaks centered at 399.8 eV and 400.8 eV, which were attributed to the CNC and NH. XPS identified surface components of N-CDs were consistent with FTIR results.

The optical properties of N-CDs were investigated by UV–vis and PL spectrum. The N-CDs showed a broad absorption spectrum in the range of 200 to 600 nm, and the peak shape was similar with the previous reported CDs [41,48]. The slight absorption shoulder at 278 nm was assigned to the π-π* transition of CC (Fig. 2A, black line), and the broad absorption band between 300 and 340 nm corresponded to n-π* transition of the CO [49,50]. The insets in Fig. 2A displayed photographs of the N-CDs obtained under visible and UV light (365 nm), respectively. When excited at 360 nm, the N-CDs showed maximum PL at around 470 nm (Fig. 2A, blue line). The optical properties of N-CDs were further explored and the excitation-dependent behavior was measured in Fig. 2B. With changing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 480 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs first increased and then decreased, and the peak position showed a red shift. This behavior was ascribed to the size effect and the distribution of the emissive sites of the N-CDs, both of which promoted the complexity of the excited states of CDs [51]. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the relative quantum yield of N-CDs was measured to be 38.7 ± 0.64% (Table S1).

The stability of the as-prepared CDs under different conditions was investigated. As revealed in Fig. 2C, PL intensities of N-CDs solution at 470 nm were enhanced when the pH was changed from 3 to 9, followed by the decrease in fluorescence intensity with further increase of pH. Since the PL intensity was higher and maintained quite constant at the physiological pH, it was beneficial to their bioimaging applications [52]. Furthermore, the effect of ionic strength on the PL stability of N-CDs was investigated in Fig. 2D. No distinct changes in PL or peak behavior in various concentrations of NaCl were observed, which was significant because it was essential for CDs to be used in the presence of physiological salt concentration in practical applications [53]. In addition, the PL intensity of N-CDs remained almost constant even after continuous irradiation under a 500 W Xenon lamp for 200 min (Fig. 2E) and storage for three months (Fig. 2F). The consequence implied that the N-CDs possessed outstanding fluorescence stability, making them promising for biological applications.

Two classes of typical free radicals (DPPH and superoxide anion radicals) were used to assess the radical scavenging activity of the N-CDs. Using 1 μg·mL−1 ascorbic acid as a positive control, the scavenging of DPPH and superoxide anion radicals were 93.8% and 99.3%, respectively (Fig. S2). DPPH is a long-lived, nitrogen containing free radical [54]. After receiving a hydrogen radical, DPPH could form steady DPPH2 complex [55]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A, the scavenging effect of N-CD towards DPPH with a concentration from 0 to 300 μg·mL−1 was investigated. With enhance concentration of the N-CDs, the absorbance of DPPH at 515 nm was gradually decreased and saturated at [N-CDs] = 210 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 3B, black line). Further addition of N-CDs could not cause obvious change in the spectrum. The final scavenging activity of N-CDs towards DPPH was as estimated as 62.8%.

Superoxide anion radicals are a kind of important cell signaling molecule involving a variety of physiological and pathological processes [56]. Changes in the content of superoxide anion radicals in vivo may lead to oxidative damage to biomolecules. Therefore, the removal of excess superoxide anion radicals is of great significance. In this work, the inhibition activity of superoxide anion radicals was measured ex vitro by pyrogallol autoxidation. Under alkaline conditions, pyrogallol could be rapidly auto-oxidized and released superoxide anion. With increasing N-CDs concentration, the inhibition of superoxide anion radicals increased and trended to saturate at [N-CDs] = 1000 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 3B, red line). Finally, the maximal superoxide anion scavenging activity of N-CDs was 81.3%. Compare the radical scavenging activity of N-CDs towards the two free radicals, it was concluded that N-CDs had better capability to remove superoxide anion radicals than to eliminate DPPH. Furthermore, the N-CDs revealed weak ability in scavenging hydroxyl radicals, as shown in Fig. S3.

Radical scavenging activity mechanism of as-prepared CDs was deduced for better understanding of its interaction. As shown in Scheme 2 , N-CDs were rich in carboxylic, amino and hydroxyl surface active groups, which might act as hydrogen donors. We assumed that both DPPH and superoxide anion radicals as hydrogen acceptors might be “grafted” at the surface of carbon dots. In this case, further oxidizing reactions of the N-CDs were expected. Above active groups were able to deliver free electrons to the N-CDs bulk structure comprising the CC backbone. Subsequently, N-CDs could be further degraded via releasing acidic residues such as carbonates and bicarbonates [57,58]. The N-CDs might eliminate free radicals in vitro by this way.

Common metal ions were chosen to assess their impact on the fluorescence emission of N-CDs under the identical conditions. The bars shown in Fig. 4A represented the fluorescence change ratio (F/F0) of the N-CDs towards the various metal ions. It was noticeable that only Fe3+ brought significantly quenching impact on the PL intensity with the lowest F/F0 value of ~0.44, whereas other metal ions showed negligible or no influence (F/F0 between 0.80 and 0.91). Moreover, UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs in the various metal ions were shown in Fig. 4B. The absorption peak of the N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ displayed apparent enhancement and had one new absorption band appeared at 282 nm in contrast to other metal ions. The relatively high selectivity of the N-CDs towards Fe3+ was attributed to the half-filled 3D orbitals, short-range ionic radius and unique coordinate interaction of Fe3+ [59]. Those properties made Fe3+ possess greater binding affinity and quicker chelating kinetics for hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CDs, which would promote the coordination reaction between N-CDs and Fe3+ [60,61]. As revealed in Fig. 4C, fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs centered at 470 nm gradually decreased when the concentration of Fe3+ was changed from 0 to 300 μM. The insets of Fig. 4C showed the visual fluorescence change of N-CDs in the absence and presence Fe3+ under UV light of 365 nm. It was obvious that the blue emission significantly reduced after adding Fe3+ to the N-CDs solution. Fig. 4D displayed a friendly linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.2–300 μM (R2 = 0.995) with a detection limit of 96.87 nM (S/N = 3).

Fluorescence quenching usually originates from static or dynamic quenching in which the fluorescent molecule reacts with the quencher [62]. The fluorescence lifetime is one of the most useful methods to distinguish dynamic and static quenching [63]. Thus, fluorescence decay traces of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ was estimated. As revealed in Fig. 5A and Table S2, fluorescence lifetime curves of both CDs and CDs/Fe3+ fitted a double-exponential function with average lifetimes of 5.99 ± 0.09 ns and 6.02 ± 0.10 ns, respectively. Consistency in their PL lifetime supported static quenching mechanism and excluded dynamic quenching. Static quenching took place by the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex or coordination between N-CDs and Fe3+ [64]. Two other evidences (absorption and zeta-potential) also supported the static quenching explanation. Fig. 5B demonstrated absorption spectrum of N-CD in the presence of Fe3+. We compared the experimental curve (absorption spectra of N-CDs + Fe3+) with the theoretical curve (the physical addition of the spectrum of N-CD and Fe3+ solution) [65]. The former did not coincide with the latter, indicating the formation of stable metal ion-CDs complexes. The zeta potential also provided the better understanding of static quenching. As shown in Table S3, the N-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) was −7.70 ± 0.31 mV. After the addition of Fe3+, the zeta potential value was shifted to −8.91 ± 0.52 mV. Thus, we presumed that Fe3+ majorly interacted with amino groups and shielded partial the positive charge, which making the zeta potential become more negative [66]. Simultaneously, little carboxyl or hydroxyl might also coordinate with Fe3+. In addition, the TEM images of N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ were examined. It was displayed that some degree of aggregation areas has formed after adding Fe3+ to the well dispersed N-CDs solution (Fig. S4). We deduced that Fe3+ coordinates with amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs to join adjacent N-CDs together to form aggregation [67]. When combined with the above results, the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs might be attributed to static quenching from Fe3+-induced aggregation of N-CDs.

Furthermore, we compared the performances of the obtained N-Ds with other green synthesized CDs (Table 1 ). It exhibited that the N-CDs possessed relatively high fluorescence quantum yield of 38.7 ± 0.64% and dual responsive properties towards Fe3+ and free radicals. The N-CDs sensing system towards Fe3+ displayed superior sensitivity when compared to others.

The cytotoxicity of the CDs was estimated by using a standard MTT assay with SMMC-7721 cells. As shown in Fig. 6 , the survival rates were higher than 85% even under a high concentration (0.85 mg·mL−1) of N-CDs. This observation confirmed the low cytotoxicity of the N-CDs and inferred their potential use in cellular imaging. The bright-field images of the SMMC-7721 cells incubated with N-CDs (Fig. 7A) clearly indicated the normal morphology of the cells, verifying that N-CDs possessed scarce toxicity. Fig. 7B–D exhibited bright blue, green, and red fluorescence when excited with 405 nm, 488 nm, and 543 nm laser, respectively. It revealed that the emission was mainly from the cytoplasmic area, demonstrating that the N-CDs exhibited good cell permeability and efficiently accumulated in the cell cytoplasmic [68]. To further investigate their applicability for monitoring intracellular Fe3+ levels, exogenous Fe3+ was introduced into the N-CDs-pretreated SMMC-7721 cells. The dynamic fluorescence imaging in the absence or presence of Fe3+ were performed to observe quenching process in real time. As shown in Fig. S5 A–D, blue fluorescence emission could be clearly observed and there were no reductions in PL intensity even after excitation for a prolonged time. After addition of the Fe3+, the fluorescence brightness decreased gradually with extended time (Fig. S5 E–H). It indicated that Fe3+ gradually entered the cells and coordinate with the intracellular N-CDs, resulting in decreases of the blue emission. In addition, we also explored the semi-quantitative determination for Fe3+ of N-CDs on SMMC-7721 cells. As shown in Fig. 7E–H, the blue emissions diminished with increasing concentration of Fe3+ in the cells. Thus, the N-CDs could serve as an effective probe for intracellular Fe3+ sensing.

In summary, we have developed a simple and eco-friendly strategy to fabricate CDs from black soya beans. The facile preparation of CDs was realized with photoluminescence QY of 38.7 ± 0.64%. Furthermore, the N-CDs possessed dual responsive properties towards free radical and Fe3+. The N-CDs exhibited ideal radical scavenging activity against DPPH and superoxide anion radicals. Meanwhile, Fe3+ could induce fluorescence quenching of N-CDs with static quenching characteristics. The N-CDs were extended to imaging intracellular Fe3+. We present that the N-CDs would hold great promise for practical application in free radical scavenging, sensing and cellular imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475080 and 21575084) and Research Project Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2017-Key1). We appreciate Scientific Instrument Center of Shanxi University for us to identify samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental instrumentations, quantum yield measurement, size distribution histograms, scavenging ability of free radical, TEM image, dynamic cell imaging, lifetime, Zeta-potential can be found in the supporting information. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.12.034.
